description: This figure presents the room-temperature microhardness (HU, unit: N/mm²) of (111)-oriented Fe-16at.%Al monocrystals under different prior heat treatments. The x-axis lists all tested treatment conditions: as-grown, 1100°C furnace cooled to room temperature (1100°C FC RT), 1100°C furnace cooled to 600°C followed by water quench (1100°C FC600°CQ), 250°C annealing for 14 days, 300°C annealing for 6 h, 300°C annealing for 3 days, 300°C annealing for 14 days, 320°C annealing for 233 days, 400°C annealing for 6 h, 600°C annealing for 6 h, water quench from 1100°C (Q*), and Q* followed by 300°C annealing for 14 days. The HU values vary by less than 10% overall: most samples show HU between 1950 and 2000 N/mm², the Q* sample has the highest HU around 2100 N/mm², while samples after medium-temperature long-time annealing such as 300°C/14d show slightly lower HU of 1870-1950 N/mm². The figure supports the conclusion that short-range ordering has no significant effect on the hardness of this alloy, and excess vacancies introduced by quenching are the main contributor to hardening, with slow kinetics of vacancy elimination during post-quench annealing.

This figure presents room-temperature compressive stress-strain curves of [112]-oriented Fe-16at.%Al monocrystals, tested at a compression rate of 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹, for samples with four different prior heat treatments: as-grown, 1100 °C furnace cooling (FC) to room temperature, 1100 °C FC to 600 °C followed by water quench, and 1100 °C water quench followed by 300 °C annealing for 14 days. All curves show similar elastic deformation behavior at low strain, followed by plastic deformation with continuous work hardening up to ~5.5% strain. The yield and flow stresses of the as-grown, 1100 °C FC, and 1100 °C FC 600 °C Q samples are nearly identical, ranging from ~430 MPa to 450 MPa, while the 1100 °C Q + 300 °C/14d sample exhibits the highest yield strength (~490 MPa) and highest flow stress across all tested strain levels. The results support the conclusion that only heat treatments at temperatures above 600 °C significantly affect the mechanical behavior of Fe-Al alloys.

This figure plots the compressive yield stress of as-cast polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys as a function of Al content (0 to 18 at.%) at room temperature, tested at two strain rates: 10⁻² s⁻¹ (open circles) and 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ (solid circles). The Fe-18at.%Al alloy (triangle marker) exhibits yielding accompanied by deformation twinning at both strain rates. Yield stress increases linearly with rising Al content for both test conditions, with a hardening coefficient of 21 MPa per at.% Al for the higher strain rate and 23 MPa per at.% Al for the lower strain rate, as indicated by the interpolated linear fitting lines. This linear dependence matches Suzuki’s solid-solution hardening theory for bcc alloys, confirming that the observed strengthening in this Fe-Al alloy system is dominated by Al solid solution strengthening.

This figure presents tensile stress-strain curves of as-cast polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys with various Al contents (4at.%, 11at.%, 14at.%, 16at.%, 17at.%, 18at.%), tested at a strain rate of 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ at two temperatures: room temperature (RT, first subplot) and -100 °C (second subplot). The curves reveal that increasing Al content consistently improves the yield strength and ultimate tensile strength of the alloys, while reducing their fracture strain. Compared to room temperature, all tested alloys exhibit higher strength at -100 °C, and alloys with higher Al content show more significant brittle fracture behavior at low temperature. Distinct serrations are observed for Fe-18at.%Al at -100 °C, which correspond to deformation twinning, while a clear yield stress drop occurs for Fe-4at.%Al at -100 °C. The figure supports the conclusion that Al addition strengthens Fe-Al alloys but reduces ductility, and low temperature further enhances brittleness, induces yield drop in low-Al alloys, and promotes deformation twinning in alloys with higher Al content.

This set of plots illustrates tensile mechanical properties of as-cast polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys (Al content 4 to 18 at.%) tested at 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ strain rate as a function of Al content: subplot (a) presents data at room temperature (RT), subplot (b) presents data at -100°C, with additional deformation twinning initiation stress data for -100°C included in (b). Measured properties cover tensile yield stress, ultimate tensile strength, strain before necking, and fracture strain. Key trends: yield stress and ultimate tensile strength increase linearly with rising Al content, with values at -100°C approximately 80 MPa higher than those at RT; ductility metrics (strain before necking, fracture strain) decrease with increasing Al content, with fracture strain dropping steeply to match strain before necking above 15 at.% Al at RT and above 12 at.% Al at -100°C, corresponding to brittle fracture without prior necking. Deformation twinning is observed at -100°C for alloys with Al ≥ 11 at.%. This figure confirms that solid-solution hardening dominates strength evolution of the alloys, while ductility reduces with higher Al content and lower testing temperature, and low-temperature twinning correlates with earlier brittle transition.

This figure contains two transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs showing dislocation distributions of polycrystalline Fe-12at.%Al alloy with 60 ppm C impurity after 5.5% compressive straining at room temperature with a strain rate of 10^-4 s^-1, following 1100 °C heat treatment. Subfigure (a) corresponds to the quenched sample, where glide bands with dislocation tangles between the bands are observed. Subfigure (b) corresponds to the furnace-cooled sample, where dislocation cell formation is initiated after deformation. Both micrographs share the same scale bar of 0.4 μm. The apparent difference in dislocation configurations demonstrates that cooling rate after high temperature heat treatment significantly affects the deformation microstructure of Fe-Al alloys, supporting that thermal processing history regulates the deformation behavior of this alloy system.

This set of transmission electron micrographs presents dislocation distributions in polycrystalline Fe-16at.%Al alloy with less than 20 ppm carbon impurity. All samples were first heat-treated at 1100 °C, then experienced 5.5% compressive straining at room temperature with a strain rate of 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. Subfigure (a) displays the quenched sample, where glide bands in various directions are observed, with mostly straight dislocations distributed between glide bands. Subfigure (b) displays the furnace-cooled sample, where obvious dislocation tangles are formed between glide bands. Both micrographs have a scale bar of 0.2 μm. This figure demonstrates that cooling rate after high-temperature heat treatment significantly alters the post-deformation dislocation configuration of Fe-16Al alloy, indicating that cooling-rate-regulated microstructural features such as excess vacancies modify dislocation movement and accumulation behaviors, which accounts for the varied deformation performance of alloys with different cooling histories.
